serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup), equivalent to 250 g, as typically served in Singaporean bubble tea or specialty tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Milk Tea (No Topping) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Milk Tea (No Topping), CR ID D08010056. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey milk tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) honey GI ~58–60 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008), (2) full-cream milk GI ~27–34, (3) black tea GI negligible. The dominant glycaemic driver is honey (liquid sugar, predominantly fructose-glucose). Blended GI estimated at ~65 reflecting the honey-dominant sugar load in a liquid matrix, which accelerates absorption. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (65 × 11.06 × 2.5) / 100 = 18.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) in a liquid sugar-sweetened beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention independently elevates blood pressure regardless of the low sodium content (13 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid honey milk tea; the 11 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, raising blood pressure independently of dietary sodium (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.06 g sugar/100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; honey and milk sugar in liquid form cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation with no fibre buffer to slow absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid honey milk tea; the 11 g/100 g liquid sugar load (GI ~65, GL ~18 per 250 ml serving) causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes, and the absence of dietary fibre provides no glycaemic buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (2.41 g/100 g, likely from non-dairy creamer or milk) combined with high sugar (11.06 g/100 g); regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit honey milk tea; the high sugar content (11 g/100 g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and the fat component (2.41 g/100 g, potentially from non-dairy creamer containing saturated or trans fats) may further elevate LDL cholesterol.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (11.06 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, no meaningful polyphenols); high sugar intake is associated with elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid honey milk tea; the high liquid sugar load promotes elevated inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and the non-dairy creamer component may contain pro-inflammatory saturated or palm oil fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Milk Tea (No Topping) is a freshly prepared Singaporean specialty tea beverage made by combining brewed black tea with milk (or non-dairy creamer) and honey, served without any additional toppings such as pearls, grass jelly, or pudding. The drink has a smooth, mildly sweet flavour profile with the floral sweetness of honey complementing the tannin notes of black tea and the creaminess of milk. Per 100 g, it provides 65 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 11.06 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 13 mg sodium. The sugar content is entirely from honey and any added sweeteners, making this a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by nutritional classification. The absence of dietary fibre means the liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, producing a significant postprandial glycaemic response (estimated GI ~65, GL ~18 per 250 ml serving). Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glucose elevation, unsuitable for hypertension management due to sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention, and of moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to the combined sugar and fat load. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or afternoon beverage in Singapore's hawker centres and bubble tea shops. The no-topping variant is lower in total calories than versions with pearls or jelly but retains the full sugar load of the base drink.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D08010056) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is the primary canonical source for Singaporean food records under Project Veritas v2. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact record without adaptation.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific honey milk tea preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010056 as of 2026-04-16. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g). GI estimated at 65 (medium) using analogous honey GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for honey milk tea exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (65 × 11.06 × 2.5) / 100 = 18.0 g. The fat content (2.41 g/100 g) likely derives from milk or non-dairy creamer; saturated fat breakdown not available in source. This beverage qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions (sugar ≥ 5 g/100 g in a beverage). Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311171 (milk tea) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (tea-based beverages with milk) used as closest defensible matches; no honey milk tea-specific codes exist in either system.
